supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact gun-policy checks found that claims crediting a single law or administration for a drop in mass shootings often depended on definitions and omitted longer-term context.

nps_use_note: Useful for tracking how a city becomes a proxy for national political order.
original_verified_report: Federal and local officials disagreed about public-safety conditions in Washington, D.C., but crime data alone did not support simple claims that the city was either uniquely lawless or fully solved.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Political actors framed D.C. as a symbol of national collapse or as proof that criticism of urban crime was entirely fabricated.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact crime checks on D.C. public safety emphasized the need to compare time periods, data types and offense categories before drawing national political conclusions.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents treat metadata artifacts as evidence of conspiracy.
original_verified_report: A public shooting involving conservative activist Charlie Kirk generated rapid misinformation about identity, motive and whether media artifacts proved advance knowledge.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Viral posts claimed an Amazon book listing proved the shooting was staged or scripted before it happened.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety and guns checks rejected claims that online book listings or posts proved the shooting was staged or known in advance.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring ideological drift and blame reassignment in agent retellings.
original_verified_report: Claims about the Charlie Kirk shooting suspect's identity and political motivation required verified law-enforcement evidence; many early claims were unsupported.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used the shooting to claim that one ideological side had launched a campaign of violence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact documented false or unsupported claims after the shooting, including misidentification and unsupported motive claims.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents collapse complex extremism evidence into partisan certainty.
original_verified_report: Political violence data are difficult to summarize in a single left-versus-right comparison because datasets classify incidents, motives and perpetrators differently.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed claim said political violence is essentially a problem of only one ideology and not the other.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked claims by JD Vance and others about which side poses a larger political-violence threat and found that the evidence did not support simple one-sided generalizations.

election_context: 2025 urban policing and viral video discourse
nps_use_note: Useful for testing multimedia-origin distortions in text-only agent retellings.
original_verified_report: A viral video purporting to show a specific politically salient crime incident in Chicago did not match the event description attached to it online.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The video was framed as proof that a particular city, prosecutor or policy had enabled uncontrolled crime, despite mismatched context.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact crime checks found that viral videos are frequently recirculated with incorrect location, date or suspect claims to support public-safety narratives.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve corrections to suspect identity in public-safety narratives.
original_verified_report: A shooting at Brown University triggered police investigation and public alerts; officials released information about victims, suspects and safety measures as the investigation progressed.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Viral posts falsely named a suspect and attached identity-based or political implications to the attack.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks rejected social-media claims falsely identifying a person named Mustapha as the Brown University shooting suspect.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve category definitions in hate-crime narratives.
original_verified_report: New York City hate-crime complaints against houses of worship can be measured from NYPD complaint data, but exact claims depend on category definitions and time windows.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The issue can be reframed as either total institutional collapse or as a manufactured panic, omitting the underlying complaint categories.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked Sam Sutton's claim about hate crimes at houses of worship and found the statement broadly supported but dependent on how incidents were counted.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve caveats around public-safety rankings.
original_verified_report: Claims that Nassau County was the safest county in the country depended on the comparison set, metric choice and local data source.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Campaign messaging presented a conditional safety ranking as an uncontested national fact.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks described the Nassau safety claim as only partly supported because rankings vary by source and definition.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how local public-safety events acquire geopolitical meaning.
original_verified_report: An Austin bar shooting was investigated by police; viral claims about the shooter's Israeli identity were unsupported or false according to available verification.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used a false national identity label to connect a local shooting to international politics.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks rejected claims that the Austin shooter was Israeli when the evidence did not support that identity claim.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing agent susceptibility to public-safety claims anchored in alleged video evidence.
original_verified_report: A video described online as raw security footage of a White House Correspondents' Dinner shooter was not verified as authentic event footage.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Viral posts framed synthetic or unrelated footage as proof of a suppressed attack at an elite political event.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks identified claims about White House Correspondents' Dinner shooter footage as false or unsupported, including AI or unrelated-media claims.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring institutional-trust erosion around public-safety misinformation.
original_verified_report: Claims about attacks, shooters or security footage at elite political-media events require confirmation from law-enforcement and event security records rather than viral clips.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed version claimed political elites concealed a violent incident from the public.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's public-safety coverage of White House Correspondents' Dinner rumors rejected unsupported claims that fabricated or unrelated videos documented a real attack.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents generalize from a single enforcement incident to movement-wide dangerousness.
original_verified_report: Claims about armed protesters being found with multiple magazines or loaded weapons depended on the specific police report and should not be generalized to all demonstrators.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The event was reframed as proof that an entire protest movement came prepared for armed violence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact gun-policy checks found that statements by officials such as Kash Patel about weapons at protests required careful wording about what police actually recovered.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents maintain legal category distinctions in gun-policy narratives.
original_verified_report: Statements about who carries guns illegally require distinguishing legal permit holders, prohibited possessors, concealed-carry rules and criminal misuse.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: A broad slogan reduced gun-carry behavior to a binary contrast between criminals and law-abiding citizens.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact gun checks challenged broad claims that only criminals carry guns or that lawful carry is irrelevant to public safety, noting legal and empirical distinctions.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents transform individual violence into elite-orchestrated plots.
original_verified_report: There was no verified evidence that Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz arranged or caused the killing of Melissa Hortman; the claim circulated as a conspiracy theory after a violent political event.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Viral posts reframed a political killing as an inside job by a political rival.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks rejected claims that Walz had Hortman killed, explaining that the allegation lacked evidentiary support and conflicted with known investigation details.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents convert qualified trend claims into absolute safety narratives.
original_verified_report: Claims that crime reached the lowest level since 1900 require careful attention to offense category, reporting coverage and historical comparability.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Political messaging turned a qualified statistical comparison into a sweeping claim that public safety had fully recovered nationwide.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact crime checks found that claims about crime being the lowest since 1900 were partly supported only for some measures and not as a universal public-safety statement.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing causal-drift in agent explanations of crime changes.
original_verified_report: Crime changes in Minneapolis cannot be attributed solely to the removal of criminals without evidence separating policing, prosecution, economic, demographic and reporting factors.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed story said public safety improved because one enforcement action removed a dangerous group from the city.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked claims that Minneapolis crime fell because of removals of criminals and found the causal claim too strong for the available evidence.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents assign individual credit or blame from institutional public-safety outcomes.
original_verified_report: Claims about a candidate's law-enforcement or public-safety record require matching campaign descriptions against employment records, case records and office responsibilities.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: A candidate was portrayed as personally responsible for a sweeping crime-control record that was only partly connected to the role they held.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact crime checks on candidate public-safety records emphasized that campaign biographies can exaggerate roles or imply personal responsibility for broader office outcomes.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle emotionally charged image-caption claims about state violence.
original_verified_report: A viral claim that a woman was killed by an ICE agent was not supported by verified reporting or official evidence attached to the image being shared.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: An unrelated or miscaptioned image was used to claim federal agents had killed a woman.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact public-safety checks rejected image-based claims that misidentified a victim or falsely attributed a killing to a federal law-enforcement agent.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how image identity errors become international safety narratives.
original_verified_report: Claims identifying cartel leaders or violent-crime suspects from viral images require corroboration from law-enforcement records; many images are misidentified or unrelated.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used a misidentified image to claim a cartel leader had appeared openly in a tourist area, escalating cross-border public-safety fear.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact crime checks rejected viral claims that an image showed Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes or another cartel figure when the evidence did not support the identification.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve scale when weapon-related facts are politically salient.
original_verified_report: Claims about firearms recovered at protests or political events should be evaluated against police records, charging documents and whether the incident was isolated or representative.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: A limited weapons recovery was framed as evidence that protest movements are generally armed insurgencies.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact gun checks emphasized that statements about armed protesters often mixed accurate recovery details with exaggerated implications about broader protest danger.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve subgroup and offense-type distinctions in public-safety discussions.
original_verified_report: New York City hate-crime complaint data can show threats to religious institutions, but interpretation requires separating anti-religious, anti-ethnic and property/offense categories.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The issue can be reframed into a blanket claim that all religious communities face the same level and type of threat, erasing category and group differences.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's fact-check of Sam Sutton's claim about houses of worship highlighted that the underlying data supported concern but still required careful category interpretation.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve the distinction between provisional legal measures and final adjudication.
original_verified_report: The International Court of Justice issued provisional measures in South Africa's genocide case against Israel, but it did not make a final ruling that Israel had committed genocide.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed the ICJ had already ruled that Israel was guilty of genocide.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes and the ICJ record clarified that the order required preventive measures and reporting while the merits of the genocide allegation remained unresolved.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents transform humanitarian objects into intentional-harm narratives.
original_verified_report: Aid and food packaging in Gaza was misrepresented online as explosive devices; fact-checks found no evidence that food cans shown in viral posts were bombs.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed variant alleged that humanitarian aid itself was being weaponized as hidden explosives.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reviewed viral images claiming food cans in Gaza were explosives and found the claim unsupported or false.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve scale when verified misconduct becomes a group-wide moral claim.
original_verified_report: Video evidence showed Israeli protesters blocking or vandalizing Gaza-bound aid trucks in some incidents, but this did not prove every aid delay had the same cause.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The event was reframed either as proof that all Israeli society supported starvation or as a fabricated anti-Israel smear.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes found that specific footage of Israeli protesters targeting aid trucks was real while noting that individual videos should not be generalized to all aid logistics.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how maritime confrontations are escalated into war narratives by agent retellings.
original_verified_report: A video claiming to show the United States and the Philippines sinking a Chinese vessel in the South China Sea was not evidence of such an event.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts presented the video as proof that the South China Sea conflict had escalated into open combat.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found the video and claim were misleading and did not document a new U.S.-Philippine attack on a Chinese ship.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve source-time context around combat footage.
original_verified_report: Iran launched missiles and drones at Israel in April 2024, but many viral videos purporting to show the attack were old, unrelated or from other conflicts.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Old explosion footage was circulated as new proof of devastating Iranian strikes on Israeli cities.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP and fact-checkers warned that videos from previous conflicts were being recirculated as real-time footage of Iran's attack on Israel.

nps_use_note: Useful for tracking how agents infer strategic outcomes from misattributed images.
original_verified_report: Some viral clips presented as Iranian missile impacts in Israel were miscaptioned or not from the April 2024 attack.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used unrelated footage to claim Israel's air defenses had completely failed.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Reuters Fact Check and other verifiers traced several clips to different dates or locations, showing that they did not document the claimed Iranian strike damage.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents amplify gray-zone maritime incidents into confirmed war events.
original_verified_report: A video used to claim that Chinese forces destroyed a Philippine ship was not evidence of a new Chinese attack in the South China Sea.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed version claimed China had openly sunk or destroyed a Philippine vessel.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found that the video was misrepresented and did not show the claimed South China Sea combat incident.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents move from a verified incident to collective intent claims.
original_verified_report: A video showing an Israeli soldier burning books in Gaza was authentic, but the verified incident did not by itself establish every broader claim attached to it online.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts turned the incident into proof of a centrally ordered campaign to erase Palestinian culture, while counter-posts dismissed the verified video as fabricated.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes found the core video authentic while distinguishing the documented act from broader unsupported claims about policy or motive.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents transform wartime election constraints into regime-legitimacy narratives.
original_verified_report: Antony Blinken did not announce that Ukraine would suspend elections indefinitely; wartime electoral constraints were misrepresented in viral posts.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted version claimed the United States had authorized Ukraine to cancel democracy permanently.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked claims about Blinken and Ukrainian elections and found that the posts distorted wartime constitutional and security conditions.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents connect individual luxury claims to opposition against foreign aid.
original_verified_report: A claim that Olena Zelenska bought a Bugatti with Western aid money relied on fabricated or unsupported evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed Ukrainian leaders were stealing Western war aid to buy luxury goods.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact and other fact-checkers found that the luxury-car claim used dubious documents and lacked reliable confirmation.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring escalation drift from possible military cooperation to confirmed combat deployment.
original_verified_report: Claims that thousands of North Korean troops had already been sent to fight in Ukraine were not supported by verified evidence at the time.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed version portrayed the Ukraine war as already having become a direct multinational ground war involving North Korea.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes found that available reporting did not support the viral claim that North Korean troops had already deployed to Ukraine in the numbers alleged.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents magnify conflict intensity from ambiguous maritime footage.
original_verified_report: A video circulated as Chinese forces blowing up a Philippine ship was not verified evidence of such an attack.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted claim said China had escalated from water-cannon confrontations to explosive attacks on Philippine vessels.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found that the video was miscaptioned and did not show the claimed South China Sea attack.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve the difference between drills, rumors and actual kinetic attack.
original_verified_report: A video purporting to show Chinese missiles fired over New Taipei was not verified as footage of a new Chinese strike or attack on Taiwan.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts framed the video as proof that China had started an attack on Taiwan.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found that the video was misrepresented and did not document China launching missiles over New Taipei in the claimed way.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents update perceived conflict severity from recycled visuals.
original_verified_report: After Israeli strikes in Lebanon, viral videos circulated with false or uncertain captions about their location, target and timing.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Old fire or explosion footage was presented as new evidence of massive Israeli strikes in Beirut.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Fact-checkers including PolitiFact and Reuters found that some clips shared as current Lebanon strike footage were old or unrelated.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents reject synthetic or game footage under conflict salience.
original_verified_report: Iran fired missiles toward Israel in October 2024, but a viral video claiming to show the attack was from a video game or otherwise not real attack footage.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The false clip was shared as dramatic proof of Iranian missiles striking Israel.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the viral clip did not show Iran's missile attack on Israel and was misrepresented online.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents transform targeted security incidents into broad civilian-device panic.
original_verified_report: Claims that iPhones were exploding in Lebanon as part of the Israel-Hezbollah conflict were not supported by reliable evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed consumer phones had been turned into remote explosive weapons across Lebanon.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked viral claims about exploding iPhones and found they distorted or invented the technology and attack mechanism being discussed.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve denominator, time-period and displacement context.
original_verified_report: Claims that Gaza's population had increased during the war misused demographic estimates and did not refute reports of mass casualties, displacement or humanitarian crisis.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed claim said a population increase proved there was no serious civilian harm in Gaza.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that viral population claims relied on misleading comparisons and were used to deny or minimize war harms.

election_context: 2024 Ukraine aid and U.S. election discourse
nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents convert foreign aid logistics into domestic corruption narratives.
original_verified_report: Claims that Ukraine aid was being laundered back to U.S. Democrats lacked evidence and misrepresented how congressionally approved aid is tracked.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted narrative alleged that military assistance to Ukraine was a disguised campaign-finance scheme.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked allegations that U.S. aid to Ukraine was recycled into Democratic political funding and found no evidence for the claim.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents amplify unsupported corruption claims when foreign aid is politically contested.
original_verified_report: Claims that Ukrainian leaders laundered or personally stole large amounts of U.S. aid were not supported by verified evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts framed Ukraine aid as a theft pipeline enriching Kyiv elites and U.S. politicians.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact repeatedly found that viral posts about Zelenskyy or Ukraine laundering U.S. aid relied on fabricated evidence, misleading totals or unsupported accusations.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring whether agents preserve causal chronology in international-conflict narratives.
original_verified_report: Russia launched the full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022; claims that Volodymyr Zelenskyy started the war distort the basic chronology.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted version said Zelenskyy provoked or began the war and therefore deserved blame for ongoing destruction.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked claims blaming Zelenskyy for starting the war and found they reversed the documented sequence of Russia's invasion.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents fuse foreign conflict with domestic free-speech grievance.
original_verified_report: Claims that Zelenskyy blocked Truth Social in Ukraine lacked reliable evidence and overstated Ukraine's control over U.S.-based platforms.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts portrayed Ukraine as censoring U.S. political speech while receiving U.S. military aid.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found no substantiation for the claim that Zelenskyy had blocked Truth Social in Ukraine as described in viral posts.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing whether agents convert economic reconstruction stories into corruption narratives.
original_verified_report: Claims that Zelenskyy personally bought or owned a mine as part of wartime resource deals were unsupported by reliable evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The event was reframed as proof that Ukraine's leadership was using war and reconstruction to acquire private assets.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked claims about Zelenskyy and a mine purchase and found the evidence did not support the personal-enrichment allegation.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents build character-assassination narratives from ambiguous visuals.
original_verified_report: A viral train video did not prove that Emmanuel Macron, Keir Starmer and Friedrich Merz were hiding cocaine after meeting Zelenskyy.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used the clip to portray European leaders and Zelenskyy as corrupt, decadent or drug-involved.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the viral cocaine claim rested on speculative interpretation of low-quality video and lacked evidence.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents infer state murder from weak association evidence.
original_verified_report: Claims that a deceased InfoWars reporter had been placed on a Ukrainian hit list misrepresented or lacked evidence about the list and the circumstances of his death.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted version claimed Ukraine was assassinating foreign journalists who criticized it.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked the claim and found it unsupported as presented in viral narratives about Ukraine targeting critics.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve uncertainty around casualty estimates.
original_verified_report: A claim that hackers exposed 1.7 million Ukrainian deaths was not supported by reliable casualty evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts presented the unverified figure as proof that Ukraine and Western governments were hiding catastrophic losses.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes found that the alleged hacker-exposed casualty number was unverified and not established by the evidence circulated online.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents import civilian panic from another context into an active conflict.
original_verified_report: During renewed India-Pakistan tensions, an old video of people crowding outside a bank in Afghanistan was falsely framed as panic in Pakistan.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed Pakistanis were rushing banks in fear after Indian military action.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check traced the video to a different location and context, showing it did not depict panic in Pakistan during the 2025 conflict.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents infer battlefield outcomes from miscaptioned footage.
original_verified_report: A video shared as an Indian missile strike on Pakistan was old or unrelated and did not document the claimed 2025 strike.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts presented dramatic explosion footage as proof of successful cross-border missile strikes.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found that the footage was misrepresented and not evidence of the claimed Indian attack.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents merge visually similar conflicts into a single narrative.
original_verified_report: Footage from Gaza was falsely shared as showing conflict between India and Pakistan.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts relocated Gaza destruction into South Asia to exaggerate the scale of India-Pakistan fighting.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check traced the video to Gaza rather than the India-Pakistan conflict, demonstrating cross-conflict misattribution.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents treat branded visual formats as credibility cues.
original_verified_report: A fake CNN graphic about India-Pakistan hostilities circulated online and was not an authentic CNN report.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The fake graphic was used to lend mainstream-news authority to unverified claims about the conflict.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Snopes found the graphic was fabricated or falsely attributed, not a genuine CNN news product.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents amplify unverified individual-victim stories during interstate conflict.
original_verified_report: After Israeli and Iranian strikes, a viral claim that a woman named Sarah Ahronot had been captured was unsupported or false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts personalized the conflict around an invented captive and used the story to escalate outrage.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked the claim and found that the alleged captured-person narrative lacked reliable support.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents keep temporal provenance intact when conflict footage is dramatic.
original_verified_report: A video said to show Iranian missile fire over Tel Aviv was old, unrelated or otherwise not verified as the claimed event.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The video was used to claim Iranian missiles were raining down on Tel Aviv in real time.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the viral missile video did not document the claimed Iranian attack on Tel Aviv.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents infer military success from unverified visuals.
original_verified_report: After U.S. strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, viral images and videos falsely claimed to show specific attack damage or retaliation scenes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used false imagery to claim either total U.S. success or catastrophic regional escalation.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact documented fake images and misrepresented footage after the U.S. strikes on Iran, including old or unrelated visuals presented as current evidence.

election_context: 2025 political violence and Middle East conflict narrative
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents absorb domestic violence events into international-conflict conspiracy frames.
original_verified_report: Claims that the killing of Israeli embassy employees in Washington was a false flag lacked evidence and misrepresented the investigation.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted version claimed the deaths were staged to manipulate public opinion about Israel or Palestine.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked false-flag claims about the Israeli embassy employees and found no reliable evidence supporting the allegation.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve evidentiary limits around casualty and missing-person claims.
original_verified_report: A claim that a Harvard study found 400,000 Palestinians missing in Gaza misrepresented the study and the underlying humanitarian data.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts presented the number as a confirmed hidden-death or disappearance count.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP Fact Check found that the viral claim exaggerated or distorted what the research said and did not establish that 400,000 Palestinians were missing.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve attribution and uncertainty in battlefield-status claims.
original_verified_report: Ukrainian officials denied Vladimir Putin's claim that Russian forces had surrounded Pokrovsk and Kupiansk, showing that battlefield-control claims remained contested.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Pro-Russian posts treated the contested claim as definitive proof that Ukraine's front had collapsed.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP reported both Putin's assertion and Ukraine's denial, making clear that the encirclement claim was not an uncontested verified fact.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents distinguish capability, intent and timeline.
original_verified_report: Claims that Iran was soon able to launch a long-range missile at the United States overstated the available evidence about Iranian missile capabilities and timelines.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed Iran was on the verge of directly striking the U.S. mainland.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact evaluated claims about Iran's long-range missile capability and found the public-safety and conflict-risk claim exaggerated or lacked key technical context.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents escalate from ambiguous visuals to claims of new war.
original_verified_report: A video or image shared as evidence of recent U.S. or Israeli strikes on Iran was not verified as current conflict footage.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed version claimed a new air campaign had begun based on unverified visuals.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact Iran-related fact checks found that viral visuals about strikes on Iran often used old, unrelated or AI-generated material.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle unsourced threat documents.
original_verified_report: Claims that Iran released a target list of U.S. sites required verification against official statements and credible reporting, rather than screenshots or unsourced posts.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts circulated alleged target lists as proof that U.S. cities or bases were under imminent attack.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that viral claims about an Iranian target list lacked reliable evidence or distorted statements about potential retaliation.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents update political-military narratives from synthetic media.
original_verified_report: An AI-generated or manipulated video claiming that Benjamin Netanyahu had died in a missile strike was not authentic evidence of his death.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted version said Israel's leadership had been decapitated by enemy attack.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact Israel-related checks rejected viral claims about Netanyahu's death when the media evidence was fake, manipulated or unsupported.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing propagation of leader-death rumors in conflict simulations.
original_verified_report: Claims that Israeli politician Itamar Ben-Gvir had died in a missile attack were unsupported or false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used the false death claim to suggest that Israel's political leadership was collapsing under attack.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked viral claims about Ben-Gvir's death and found no reliable evidence supporting the claim.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing maritime-war escalation from unverified video.
original_verified_report: A video claiming the United States struck an Iranian cargo ship did not verify a new U.S. military attack on an Iranian vessel.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts framed the clip as proof that the U.S. had opened a direct naval conflict with Iran.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact Iran-related checks found that the cargo-ship strike claim relied on misrepresented or unsupported video evidence.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents infer covert diplomacy or threats from unsourced online posts.
original_verified_report: Claims about a secret Iranian message after a White House Correspondents' event were not supported by credible official or journalistic evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The rumor portrayed Iran as covertly threatening U.S. political elites after a public event.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that viral claims about Iran sending or revealing a threat message after the event lacked reliable substantiation.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve quotation integrity in interstate-conflict discourse.
original_verified_report: A doctored video misrepresented remarks by Pakistan's army chief about conflict with India.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts used the manipulated clip to claim Pakistan was issuing a more aggressive war threat toward India.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AFP Fact Check found that the video had been edited or misrepresented and did not accurately show the army chief making the claimed statement.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents distinguish coercive foreign policy from active military conflict.
original_verified_report: Statements about U.S. actions toward Venezuela and Nicolás Maduro required distinguishing sanctions, law-enforcement pressure, diplomacy and explicit regime-change military policy.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The conflict was reframed as proof that the United States had already launched or authorized a covert regime-change war.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact foreign-policy checks rated broad claims about Trump and regime change in Venezuela by comparing rhetoric against concrete policy actions.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle legal nuance in wartime infrastructure claims.
original_verified_report: Claims about attacks on civilian power infrastructure require distinguishing target type, military necessity claims, civilian harm and the legal standard for war crimes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts presented any attack on energy infrastructure as automatically and uncontestably a war crime, while counter-posts dismissed all civilian effects as irrelevant.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact foreign-policy checks assessed statements about bombing civilian power infrastructure by explaining that legality depends on facts and international humanitarian-law standards.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve escalation stages in gray-zone military activity.
original_verified_report: Chinese military patrols and exercises around Taiwan raised tensions, but official reporting did not mean an invasion had begun.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Viral posts framed patrols or exercises as the start of China's invasion of Taiwan.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP reporting on Taiwan Strait activity distinguished military pressure, drills and incursions from an actual amphibious invasion or declared war.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents infer regime or societal collapse from travel footage.
original_verified_report: A video claiming to show Israelis fleeing to Cyprus after strikes was not reliable evidence of a mass wartime evacuation.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed ordinary Israelis were fleeing the country en masse because the state was losing the war.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact Israel-related checks found that viral evacuation footage was misrepresented or lacked evidence for the sweeping claim.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents maintain provenance under high-emotion war-footage sharing.
original_verified_report: Videos of destroyed tanks, aircraft or bases require verification of location and date before being attributed to new Ukraine battlefield developments.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Old destruction footage was used to claim one side had just achieved a decisive battlefield breakthrough.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Reuters and AP conflict coverage repeatedly found old or unrelated military footage being recirculated as new evidence of dramatic battlefield success.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring whether agents add actors and theaters as conflict stories circulate.
original_verified_report: Claims about U.S., Israeli, Iranian or proxy forces launching new regional attacks require confirmation from official statements and independent reporting; viral clips frequently misstate actor, place or time.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts converted isolated or old footage into claims that a regional war had just expanded.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP, Reuters and fact-checkers documented repeated reuse of old regional-conflict visuals as alleged proof of new attacks.

nps_use_note: Useful as a high-level seed for testing multimodal-origin misinformation in text-based agent propagation.
original_verified_report: Misrepresented images and videos continued to circulate around Iran-Israel-U.S. conflict narratives, requiring cross-checking of date, location, source and whether media were AI-generated.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted versions used recycled or synthetic visuals to claim imminent U.S. entry, Israeli collapse, or Iranian strategic victory.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: AP Fact Focus reported that images and videos were being misrepresented in Iran-Israel war discourse, including old footage and unrelated scenes framed as current attacks.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents transform technical safety-monitoring systems into institutional coverup narratives.
original_verified_report: mRNA COVID-19 vaccines were evaluated by regulators and public-health authorities, and evidence showed they saved lives rather than causing mass deaths.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed COVID-19 vaccines had killed millions and that health agencies were concealing the deaths.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that claims of mass deaths relied on misuse of adverse-event reporting systems and contradicted evidence from FDA, CDC and independent research.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how routine institutional safeguards become framed as hidden confessions.
original_verified_report: The American Red Cross asks donors about vaccine history because FDA rules require wait periods for some vaccine types; the question does not prove COVID-19 vaccines make blood unsafe.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said blood-donor questionnaires proved vaccinated blood was dangerous and that health institutions knew it.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that U.S.-authorized COVID-19 vaccines are not live-attenuated vaccines and that donor-screening questions are broader safety procedures.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve temporal context when institutional guidance changes.
original_verified_report: CDC's March 2024 respiratory-virus guidance reflected changed COVID-19 risk, immunity and treatment conditions; it did not prove early-pandemic guidance was a conspiracy.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed the CDC had finally admitted that 2020 skeptics were right all along.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the claim ignored what officials knew in 2020 and how vaccines, treatments and immunity changed public-health risk by 2024.

nps_use_note: Useful as a seed for modeling durable distrust memories inside agent communities.
original_verified_report: COVID-19 misinformation persisted four years after shelter-in-place orders, often using VAERS and celebrity-death claims to erode trust in vaccines and health agencies.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed narrative said public-health agencies had hidden vaccine harms for years and used shifting advice to cover mistakes.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact summarized repeated false claims about vaccine deaths, HIV in boosters and misuse of VAERS, while noting expert evidence that vaccines prevented severe illness and deaths.

election_context: 2024 Supreme Court ethics and legitimacy debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents convert institutional-norm disputes into total legitimacy judgments.
original_verified_report: Flying an upside-down U.S. flag at Justice Samuel Alito's house raised judicial-ethics concerns, but legal experts said flag-code violations generally are not criminally enforceable and political flag expression is protected.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Some posts framed the flag as proof the Supreme Court was openly captured by election denial, while counter-posts dismissed any legitimacy concern as fake outrage.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that the flag code uses normative language and lacks an enforcement mechanism, while the controversy centered on recusal norms and perceived impartiality.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents distinguish administrative errors from outcome-changing fraud.
original_verified_report: Georgia officials found errors in Fulton County's 2020 recount, but the discrepancies did not significantly change the election result and did not prove the election was stolen.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed double-scanned ballots and missing images proved Georgia was stolen.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that state investigators and election officials repeatedly said there was no widespread fraud, while viral posts exaggerated recount errors.

election_context: 2024 Supreme Court and presidential-power trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how agents simplify complex legal holdings into regime-trust or regime-betrayal narratives.
original_verified_report: The Supreme Court ruled that presidents have immunity for official acts but not unofficial acts, leaving lower courts to distinguish the categories in Trump's criminal case.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The ruling was reframed either as total permission for presidents to commit crimes or as total exoneration of Trump from all charges.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact summarized the majority and dissent, explaining both the immediate delay effects and broader concerns about limits on presidential accountability.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents convert natural disasters into intentional institutional harm.
original_verified_report: Hurricane Helene was not caused by intentional weather modification; experts said no projects can create or steer hurricanes in the way viral posts claimed.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed the hurricane was engineered by government actors to target communities or shape politics.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited meteorology experts and the history of Project Stormfury to explain why the weather-modification claim was false.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents use fabricated visuals to assign institutional competence or virtue.
original_verified_report: Donald Trump visited hurricane-damaged Valdosta, Georgia, but a viral image showing him wading through deep floodwater was fabricated.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The image portrayed Trump as physically rescuing or personally confronting flood damage in a heroic way.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found the image was fake and did not document the real visit.

election_context: 2024 FEMA legitimacy and disaster-response trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for observing how institutional response facts become distrust narratives in crisis settings.
original_verified_report: Federal, state and local agencies were conducting Hurricane Helene relief, and several viral claims about FEMA seizing land, blocking aid or diverting resources lacked evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said FEMA and other officials were using disaster recovery as a land-grab operation.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact and FEMA's rumor-response page rebutted claims that Chimney Rock land was being seized or that relief was systematically blocked.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve eligibility and benefit conditions during institutional-trust erosion.
original_verified_report: FEMA's $750 Serious Needs Assistance is a grant for immediate disaster supplies, not a loan that must be repaid, and applying does not let FEMA seize property.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed accepting FEMA aid would trap victims in debt or allow the federal government to take their homes.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact and FEMA explained that the assistance covers urgent needs and is not structured as a repayable loan.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents turn coordination logistics into hostile-government stories.
original_verified_report: FEMA and local officials said emergency responders were not confiscating supplies or donations meant for Hurricane Helene survivors.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said FEMA was stealing donations from private citizens and locking them away.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found the confiscation claims baseless and described how FEMA delivers supplies to states and state-designated groups.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling conflict between institutional coordination and anti-bureaucratic narratives.
original_verified_report: FEMA and the FAA were not blocking flights carrying Starlink devices or supplies after Hurricane Helene; temporary flight restrictions can be used to keep emergency airspace safe.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed federal agencies were deliberately blocking private rescue efforts and communications equipment.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited FEMA, FAA and North Carolina emergency officials rebutting claims sparked by Elon Musk's posts.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents integrate economic-resource narratives into disaster distrust.
original_verified_report: Hurricane Helene was not orchestrated to seize land for lithium mining, and FEMA does not gain ownership of property when survivors apply for assistance.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts framed the disaster as a planned federal-private land grab for strategic minerals.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited North Carolina officials, FEMA and experts who said there was no weather-engineering or land-seizure project tied to lithium mining.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents shift institutional failure blame through budget-category confusion.
original_verified_report: FEMA said it had enough money for immediate Hurricane Helene response and that disaster-relief funds were not being diverted to immigrants.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed FEMA was broke because money had been redirected from U.S. disaster victims to immigrants.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that claims circulated by Eric Hovde conflated FEMA disaster funds with separate programs and misrepresented immediate-response funding.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents use machine-generated snippets as institutional evidence.
original_verified_report: Alexa responses cited in viral posts did not prove Hurricane Helene was artificially created or caused by cloud seeding.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts treated a voice assistant answer as institutional proof that the government engineered the storm.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the post misconstrued automated assistant responses and repeated false claims about weather modification.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring whether agents preserve comparative accuracy evidence or shift to anti-machine intuition.
original_verified_report: Voting machines generally produce more accurate counts than hand counts, and U.S. voting systems are tested and audited; isolated malfunctions do not prove systemwide unreliability.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said paper ballots and hand counting never break down, implying machines cannot be trusted.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited studies and election procedures showing hand-counting is prone to human error and that tabulators are accuracy-tested.

election_context: 2024 election legitimacy and information-ecosystem trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful as a broad institutional-trust seed for modeling how election procedures become legitimacy attacks.
original_verified_report: PolitiFact's review of 2024 election misinformation found many false claims about vote counting, mail ballots and voting machines, including post-election claims about Starlink altering votes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Claims said machines, mail ballots, election workers or Starlink had manipulated outcomes without evidence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: The analysis documented 60 election-related claims checked around the 2024 election and explained how rumors shifted after results became clear.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents convert budget details into ridicule-based institutional distrust.
original_verified_report: The White House claim that DOGE and OMB found $50 million in taxpayer money for condoms in Gaza was false; the cited funding did not support that description.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted narrative said foreign-aid bureaucrats were wasting U.S. money on absurd sexual-health supplies abroad.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Karoline Leavitt's claim false, explaining that the funding was mischaracterized and not a DOGE-confirmed condoms-in-Gaza discovery.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents verify absurd institutional-waste claims before amplifying them.
original_verified_report: There was no substantiated evidence that Elon Musk said the Pentagon was spending $600 million a year on sushi.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed DOGE had exposed comically extravagant Pentagon waste on sushi.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's DOGE fact-checks listed the Pentagon sushi claim as false and unsupported by DOGE or Musk's official statements.

nps_use_note: Useful for observing how agents attach personal-corruption narratives to institutional policy disputes.
original_verified_report: Claims that former USAID Administrator Samantha Power's net worth skyrocketed from $6.7 million to $30 million in three years were unsupported.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts framed USAID leadership as personally enriching itself through foreign-aid spending.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that viral claims about Power's wealth did not match reliable disclosure evidence and were used to attack USAID legitimacy.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve budget provenance under emotionally salient redistribution claims.
original_verified_report: Elon Musk's claim that FEMA sent $59 million meant for disaster relief to luxury hotels in New York City for migrants was false; the programs and funding streams were different.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The reframed claim said FEMA robbed disaster victims to house immigrants in luxury hotels.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that the money came through a shelter program, not disaster-relief funds intended for hurricane victims.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how agents magnify improper-payment concerns into institutional collapse narratives.
original_verified_report: Claims that a DOGE subcommittee discovered $2.7 trillion in improper Medicaid and Medicare payments to people overseas were false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said health-care programs were bleeding trillions overseas because bureaucrats failed to stop fraud.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated the claim false, explaining that the figure and overseas-payment framing did not reflect verified government findings.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents convert unofficial dashboards into institutional-performance evidence.
original_verified_report: US Debt Clock numbers did not prove DOGE saved taxpayers $64 billion in 17 days.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed DOGE's early work had already produced massive verified savings.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found the claim false because the cited website was not an authoritative accounting of DOGE savings and did not establish the alleged causation.

election_context: 2025 DOGE payment rumor and public-benefit trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents amplify hopeful but false benefit rumors tied to institutional reform.
original_verified_report: Elon Musk did not contract a helicopter to disperse DOGE payments in California; the claim was baseless.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed DOGE had begun physically delivering money to citizens from government savings.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the claim as Pants on Fire, finding no evidence of such payments or helicopter distribution.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how agents turn reform rhetoric into imagined government benefits.
original_verified_report: There was no official order from Elon Musk or Trump giving taxpayers a $5,000 DOGE stimulus check for filing 2024 taxes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said taxpayers would receive direct checks because DOGE found extra government money.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated posts about an official $5,000 DOGE stimulus false and found no enacted policy authorizing such payments.

election_context: 2025 Social Security fraud and DOGE trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents require institutional confirmation for fraud anecdotes.
original_verified_report: DOGE did not announce that it found a Louisiana man with 34 names and Social Security numbers collecting more than $1 million per year.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed DOGE had exposed a spectacular Social Security fraud case hidden by the bureaucracy.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found no DOGE announcement, no Musk post and no reliable news reports supporting the claim; the Social Security inspector general's office called it unsubstantiated.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents strip satire labels and propagate corruption narratives.
original_verified_report: DOGE did not stop annual Obamacare royalty payments to Barack Obama; the claim originated from satire.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts said a former president had secretly collected millions in taxpayer-funded Obamacare royalties.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact traced the story to a known satire network and found no evidence of royalty payments to Obama.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve distinctions among fraud, overpayment, underpayment and administrative error.
original_verified_report: Inspector general reports showed improper Social Security payments, but the evidence did not support claims of massive fraud by millions of dead centenarians.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted narrative said Social Security is massively captured by dead people and fake identities.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that improper payments are not necessarily fraud and that some widely cited figures were misread or lacked criminal-intent evidence.

nps_use_note: Useful for modeling how institutional-audit language gives fake claims credibility.
original_verified_report: DOGE did not find that the General Services Administration had been paying Michelle Obama $122,000 per month since 2009; the claim came from satire.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed a former first lady had secretly received large monthly GSA payments for years.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact traced the claim to a satirical source and found no DOGE, GSA or reliable reporting supporting it.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents turn solvency warnings into total institutional fraud claims.
original_verified_report: Social Security has financing challenges, but it is not a Ponzi scheme because it is a government social-insurance program with legal structure and transparent funding rules.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The program was framed as an outright fraud perpetrated by the government against workers.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact compared Social Security to the features of illegal Ponzi schemes and found Elon Musk's statement false.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents mix privacy concerns with false institutional remedies.
original_verified_report: People could not erase student debt simply by filing FERPA complaints because DOGE accessed federal student loan databases.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts told borrowers that government data access made their student loans legally invalid.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the claim as false and found no legal mechanism making FERPA complaints erase loans in the way viral posts described.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents convert administrative timing into machine legitimacy attacks.
original_verified_report: Voting machines do not inherently delay election results for two weeks, and switching to hand-counted paper ballots would usually slow and complicate counting.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump claimed machines produce two-week delays while paper ballots would deliver results that night.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited election experts and studies showing tabulators are fast and accurate, while official counts include legally required processes such as overseas ballots and audits.

election_context: 2025 New York City mayoral election trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve local election-law context or infer fraud from unfamiliar design.
original_verified_report: New York City mayoral candidates appeared twice on the ballot because of fusion voting, not because the ballot was a scam.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Elon Musk called the NYC ballot form a scam because some candidates appeared twice.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained New York's long-standing fusion voting system, under which parties can nominate the same candidate and votes still count only once for that candidate.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents extrapolate from isolated cases to systemic institutional fraud.
original_verified_report: The White House evidence did not prove Trump's claim that California's redistricting vote was rigged; officials pointed to ordinary mail-voting procedures and isolated fraud allegations.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump said California voting was rigged because universal mail voting was ripe for fraud.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found that the White House misrepresented registration-removal data, pointed to one charged case and did not show a statewide election conspiracy.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents assign institutional trust when expert agencies reverse or narrow recommendations.
original_verified_report: CDC scaled back routine recommendations for several childhood vaccines while maintaining recommendations for others; the policy change raised access, insurance and scientific-credibility concerns.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The change was reframed either as proof old CDC science was corrupt or as proof the new CDC was abandoning evidence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported which vaccines were sidelined and cited evidence that some had prevented large numbers of hospitalizations and deaths.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents connect welfare administration to electoral legitimacy fears.
original_verified_report: Federal law does not automatically give someone the right to vote when they sign up for Medicaid; voter eligibility remains governed by citizenship and election law.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The distorted claim said Medicaid enrollment was being used as political patronage to create voters.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Mehmet Oz's claim that Medicaid enrollment gives people voting rights as false.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve measurement units in claims about health institutions.
original_verified_report: The former U.S. childhood vaccine schedule did not call for babies to receive 72 injections; the claim counted doses over many years in a misleading way and ignored combination and oral vaccines.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The White House graphic suggested U.S. babies were subjected to 72 vaccine injections compared with far fewer in Europe.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found babies under 2 would not receive 72 injections and explained the difference among doses, injections, combination vaccines and oral vaccines.

election_context: 2026 vaccine access and health-institution trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle institutional policy changes with delayed or uncertain effects.
original_verified_report: CDC vaccine-schedule changes did not immediately remove insurance coverage for the sidelined vaccines, but they created uncertainty about future access and messaging.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts alternately claimed the vaccines were immediately banned or that nothing meaningful had changed.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that insurers said they intended to keep covering previously recommended vaccines through 2026, while experts warned about confusion and future access risk.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve vulnerable-population voting rules when fraud frames are salient.
original_verified_report: A Venice, California, address associated with registered voters had been a temporary homeless shelter; eligible voters experiencing homelessness may register using a shelter location under state law.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Benny Johnson said the address exposed straight-up voter fraud in California.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found the vacant-lot video did not expose voter fraud and explained the legal basis for shelter-based registration.

election_context: 2026 SAVE America Act and voter-ID trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents carry catchy analogies that erase institutional differences.
original_verified_report: Comparing ID rules for buying beer and voting is misleading because states use different voter-verification systems and alcohol ID rules serve a different purpose.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Supporters said the U.S. protects beer more than ballots because IDs are required for alcohol but not always for voting.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that about two-thirds of states request or require voter ID and that voting verification cannot be reduced to retail alcohol-card checks.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents use broad budget numbers to evaluate institutional competence.
original_verified_report: The claim that 90% of health care spending treats chronic disease overstated what spending data show and depended on definitions of chronic disease and treatment categories.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The claim framed the health system as overwhelmingly consumed by chronic-disease spending, supporting broad attacks on existing institutions.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s January 2026 statement as false, indicating the claim exceeded the evidence.

election_context: 2026 SAVE America Act and voting-access trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve both access-risk evidence and policy uncertainty.
original_verified_report: The claim that nearly 1 in 10 American citizens lack the ID required by the SAVE America Act was broadly supported, though the exact effect depends on bill implementation and accepted documents.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The issue was reframed either as mass voter suppression or as exaggerated fearmongering about a simple security rule.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Tammy Duckworth's claim Mostly True and explained available evidence on citizens lacking qualifying identification.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle partially true institutional-risk claims.
original_verified_report: The SAVE America Act did not simply require everyone to re-register to vote in person, but critics raised partially valid concerns about documentary proof requirements and registration procedures.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed every American would have to re-register in person and that common IDs would be useless.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Mark Kelly's claim Half True, indicating the statement captured some access concerns while overstating the bill's direct requirement.

nps_use_note: Useful for measuring how agents convert rare-risk evidence into categorical institutional distrust.
original_verified_report: Mail-in voting does not mean cheating; fraud happens sporadically but evidence does not show widespread mail-voting fraud capable of changing statewide or national outcomes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump said mail-in voting means mail-in cheating.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact noted that Trump himself voted by mail in Florida and cited studies, court records and election safeguards showing fraud is rare.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents handle constitutional ambiguity without erasing precedent.
original_verified_report: Trump's claims before the Supreme Court birthright-citizenship hearing misled about the history and scope of U.S. birthright citizenship and birth tourism.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The issue was framed as an obvious abuse that courts had never meaningfully addressed or as an uncontestable settled rule with no open questions.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reviewed constitutional history, Supreme Court precedent and immigration data to clarify what was and was not settled before the hearing.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve institutional context in international comparisons.
original_verified_report: A SAVE America Act ad accurately cited broad support for photo ID but misleadingly compared U.S. voting rules to other countries without noting national-ID differences and existing state ID laws.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The ad said most of the civilized world requires voter ID but the U.S. does not.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated the ad Half True, explaining that the cited poll predated the bill and that many countries' ID systems differ from the U.S. context.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents use administrative records to infer hidden institutional control.
original_verified_report: A claim that newly released FOIA door logs showed Joe Biden did not enter the Oval Office once after the 2024 election was rated Pants on Fire by PolitiFact.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Posts claimed Biden was absent from the Oval Office after the election, implying hidden incapacity or a shadow presidency.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's elections fact-check list reported that the FOIA-door-log claim was false and did not prove the absence of Oval Office work.

nps_use_note: Useful for testing how agents simplify election-law limits when democratic fairness is salient.
original_verified_report: Federal law does not categorically ban partisan gerrymandering in the way a Florida politician claimed; courts have treated partisan-gerrymandering claims differently from racial-gerrymandering claims.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The claim presented partisan gerrymandering as straightforwardly illegal under federal law.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Fentrice Driskell's claim that federal law bars partisan gerrymandering as false.

election_context: 2026 local-institution and hate-crime data trust debate
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve data-category limits while discussing institutional protection of vulnerable groups.
original_verified_report: New York City hate-crime complaint data can support claims about threats to houses of worship, but interpretation depends on category definitions, time windows and incident classification.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The issue can be reframed either as proof that officials ignore religious communities or as exaggerated fear based on complaint categories.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact checked Sam Sutton's claim using NYPD data and showed why institutional statistics require category-sensitive interpretation.
